readings.title: The Elements of Computing Systems: Building a Modern Computer from First Principles (Chapters 1-3)
readings.note: Chapters 1-3 cover Boolean logic, Boolean arithmetic, and sequential logic. The Nand2Tetris approach builds registers, RAM, and a program counter from flip-flops -- exactly the progression in this week's lectures. Project 3 (Sequential Logic) is particularly relevant.

readings.title: MIT 6.004 Computation Structures: Sequential Logic and Finite State Machines (Lectures 5-6)
readings.note: Lecture 5 covers latches, flip-flops, timing constraints (setup, hold, contamination delay), and the dynamic discipline. Lecture 6 covers FSMs, Moore vs. Mealy, and metastability. The treatment of timing constraints is particularly rigorous.

readings.title: Metastability in FPGA Design: Causes, Effects, and Mitigation
readings.note: An excellent practical guide to metastability in real FPGA designs. Covers MTBF calculation, synchronizer design, and multi-clock domain crossing. The MTBF formula and synchronizer chain analysis in this week's lecture are based on this model.

readings.title: Linear Feedback Shift Registers: Theory and Applications
readings.note: Comprehensive reference on LFSR theory, including tables of primitive polynomials for all register widths up to 64 bits. Also covers applications in CRC computation and built-in self-test (BIST). The polynomial tables are invaluable for Problem Set 3.

readings.title: Digital Design and Computer Architecture: RISC-V Edition (Chapters 3-4)
readings.note: Chapter 3 covers sequential logic (latches, flip-flops, synchronizers, FSMs) and Chapter 4 covers hardware description languages. The treatment of timing analysis and FSM design methodology aligns closely with this week's lectures.
